title: Tomography of quantum states with bounded extent
abstract: We give a general framework for tomography of states that have bounded-extent with respect to a structured class of states. Let $\textsf{C}$ be a family of $n$-qubit states such that: $(i)$ $\textsf{C}$ is succinctly representable and $(ii)$ there is a weak agnostic learner of $\textsf{C}$. We give a tomography protocol for an unknown state $|ψ\rangle$ that is promised to admit a decomposition of the form $|ψ\rangle = \sum_i c_i |φ_i\rangle$, where $|φ_i\rangle \in \textsf{C}$ with bounded $\ell_1$-norm of the coefficients (which we call extent). Our main contribution is to show that a weak agnostic learner for $\textsf{C}$ can be boosted into a tomography algorithm for states with bounded extent with respect to $\textsf{C}$.   Our reduction is black-box and applies broadly across model classes. As an application, when $\textsf{C}$ is the class of stabilizer states, we obtain tomography algorithms for states with stabilizer extent $ξ$ up to trace distance $\varepsilon$, in time $\textsf{poly}(n,(ξ/\varepsilon)^{\log(ξ/\varepsilon)})$, which is improvable to $ \textsf{poly}(n,ξ,1/\varepsilon)$ assuming the algorithmic polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture in the high-doubling regime. When the unknown state $|ψ\rangle$ is arbitrary, we give an algorithmic decomposition result in the spirit of a weak regularity lemma for quantum states with respect to $\textsf{C}$ and show that the structure in $|ψ\rangle$ that is explainable by $\textsf{C}$ can be efficiently learned. Our main conceptual message is that agnostic learning of a structured base class automatically yields learnability of its low-complexity linear span.
summary: This paper establishes a general theoretical framework for performing quantum state tomography when the unknown state has a low-complexity decomposition over a structured model class. By boosting weak agnostic learners, the authors derive efficient algorithms that estimate the state with bounded error, providing significant advances over previous methods that required full access to the state preparation unitary.
main_problem: Developing a general framework for quantum state tomography when the unknown state $|\psiangle$ is assumed to have a 'bounded extent' with respect to a structured class of states $C$. This addresses efficiently learning an unknown quantum state that admits a low-complexity decomposition over $C$.
main_result: The authors provide algorithmic protocols for tomography achieving $\epsilon$-error estimation, showing that the weak agnostic learner can be boosted into a strong algorithm. For stabilizer states, this is achieved in quasipolynomial time.
method: The core method involves leveraging the existence of a weak agnostic learner for $C$ and applying an algorithmic decomposition theorem to reconstruct $|\psiangle$. This process uses techniques like ridge projection and boosting from computational learning theory.
model_or_system: The system is general quantum states, specifically focusing on $n$-qubit systems. The structure is formalized by a class of states $C$ (e.g., stabilizer states, MPS states), and the complexity is measured by the 'bounded extent' $\xi$.
key_observables: Fidelity ($F_C(|\psiangle)$), trace distance ($\epsilon$), Stabilizer extent ($\xi$).
important_parameters: Number of qubits ($n$), Extent bound ($\xi$), Error tolerance ($\epsilon$), Sample complexity, Time complexity.
main_assumptions: The results rely on $|\psiangle$ admitting a low-complexity decomposition over $C$, and the best polynomial bounds assume the algorithmic PFR conjecture.
paper_structure: The paper builds from general framework development (Chunk 1) to specific algorithms for structure learning and parameter estimation using ridge projectors (Chunk 2). It details protocols for various structured classes like stabilizer states and MPS (Chunks 3, 5, 6), while also providing rigorous mathematical analysis of the projection process and condition number bounds (Chunks 7, 8).
why_interesting: The work provides powerful tools to characterize and learn complex quantum states based on their underlying structure, which is crucial for understanding resource limitations in quantum computation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
